retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Ductus Venosus -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Researchers have noted that decreased or abnormal blood flow through the ductus venosus during the first trimester has links with aneuploidies such as trisomy 13, 18, 21, and Turner syndrome. [11] Therefore, Doppler during the first trimester is a beneficial screening tool for these conditions. Abnormal ductus venosus waveforms during atrial contraction are also associated with a three-fold increase in congenital heart defects. [12] Early fetal growth restriction can also be identified in the second trimester using ductus venosus Doppler, which shows increased shunting to preserve cerebral and cardiac blood flow. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Tricuspid Atresia -- Treatment / Management -- Patients With D-TGA and Subaortic Obstruction
retrieved_docs.content: For patients with type II lesions characterized by a VSD that obstructs aortic blood flow, the first stage may involve enlarging the VSD or implementing a Damus-Kaye-Stansel procedure (an anastomosis between the main pulmonary artery and ascending aorta) in conjunction with a modified BT shunt.

retrieved_docs.title: Transposition of the Great Arteries -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: TGA results from the failure of the aorticopulmonary septum to spiral, which can arise from defects in the maturation of the right superior and left inferior truncus swellings, improper fusion of the conus swellings with the aorticopulmonary septum, or abnormalities in neural crest development or migration. These pathological changes in d-TGA disrupt normal cardiac physiology. Because the 2 circuits run parallel, deoxygenated blood continuously recirculates through the systemic circulation, while oxygenated blood is confined to the pulmonary circuit.

retrieved_docs.title: Patent Ductus Arteriosus -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Describe features that should prompt evaluation for patent ductus arteriosus. Describe the management options for patent ductus arteriosus. Review the potential complications of patent ductus arteriosus. Explain how the interprofessional teams can work collaboratively to prevent the potentially profound complications of unrecognized or untreated patent ductus arteriosus by applying knowledge about its presentation, evaluation, and management. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Thorax, Heart Ductus Arteriosus -- Surgical Considerations
retrieved_docs.content: The ductus arteriosus can be of surgical importance when treating certain congenital heart defects. One such example is when a child is born with transposition of the great arteries. In this condition, the ascending aorta and the pulmonary trunk are switched in their locations; this leads to an inability for oxygenated blood to leave the pulmonary circulation and enter the systemic circulation. If left untreated, this condition is incompatible with life. [5] In these situations, an arterial switch is the only definitive treatment. [6] In these situations, prostaglandin E1 is often administered prophylactically to maintain the patency of the ductus arteriosus until a surgical correction can be made. This allows for blood to mix between the two distinct circulatory circuits.

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Heart Tube -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Deviation from this normal development can cause a multitude of abnormalities in the growing heart. If there is a defect in the dynein, which guides the heart into the correct position, dextrocardia will occur. Dextrocardia occurs when the heart is located on the right side of the chest instead of the left. It is usually seen in Kartagener syndrome. If the endocardial cushion, which forms the valves and septums within the heart, does not fuse there will be an open connection between the right atrium and left atrium. This connection is called a patent foramen ovale. This will lead to shifting of oxygenated blood into the right side of the heart due to the increased pressure present on the left side, as well as paradoxical emboli. Along with a patent foramen ovale, there will also be valve absorbabilities, whether they be stenotic, regurgitant, displaced or atretic. The most common displaced valve seen in early development is Ebstein's anomaly. This is when the tricuspid valve is displaced downward into the right ventricle, which can cause right-sided heart failure and tricuspid regurgitation in the future. Lithium toxicity can cause it. The neural crest and endocardial cell migration are responsible for the twisting and fusion of the truncus arteriosus and bulbus cordis into the aorticopulmonary septum, ascending aorta, and pulmonary trunk. Failure of migration leads to the transposition of great vessels, patent truncus arteriosus, and tetralogy of Fallot. Transposition of the great vessels means that the aorta leaves from the right ventricle rather than the left and the pulmonary trunk leaves from the left ventricle. This creates a complete separation of systemic and pulmonary circulations. If not, intervention is done infants will die within months. [6] [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Truncus Arteriosus -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: While the developmental underpinnings are poorly understood, truncus arteriosus is frequently associated with 22q11.2 microdeletion (Di George) syndrome in 12% to 35% of patients. Abnormalities in the secondary heart field and cardiac neural crest cells are likewise strongly implicated. [17] [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Thorax, Heart Ductus Arteriosus -- Clinical Significance -- Patent Ductus Arteriosus
retrieved_docs.content: During fetal development, prostaglandins (E1 and E2) are responsible for maintaining the patency of the ductus arteriosus. Prostaglandin E2 has the most potent effect and is produced both by the placenta and the ductus arteriosus itself. [11] A patent ductus arteriosus can also be induced by the direct administration of prostaglandin E1. This is often done to maintain circulation in children born with life-threatening congenital heart defects. One such example is when a child is born with transposition of the great vessels, a condition where the aorta and pulmonary trunk are switched in location. Ordinarily, this condition would exclude oxygenated blood from entering the systemic circulation. However, a patent ductus arteriosus allows oxygenated blood to cross over and buys the surgeon time to prepare for surgery.

retrieved_docs.title: Transposition of the Great Arteries -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Transposition of the great arteries (TGA) is a congenital cardiac defect characterized by an embryological discordance of the aorta and pulmonary trunk. In dextro-transposition of the great arteries (d-TGA), the aorta arises from the right ventricle and the pulmonary trunk from the left ventricle, resulting in 2 parallel circuits that are incompatible with life unless there is a shunt, such as a patent ductus arteriosus (PDA) or septal defect, to allow for blood mixing. Congenitally corrected TGA (ccTGA) features both atrioventricular and ventriculoarterial discordance—a condition known as "double discordance." CcTGA may be associated with additional cardiac anomalies. Early diagnosis and surgical intervention, such as the arterial switch operation, are essential for optimal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: Patent Ductus Arteriosus -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Coronary artery fistula Sinus of Valsalva aneurysm Aortopulmonary defect Persistent truncus arteriosus Pulmonary arteriovenous fistula Total anomalous pulmonary venous return

retrieved_docs.title: Patent Ductus Arteriosus -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: In most healthy newborns, the ductus will close within 12-24 hours of life. This occurs by contraction of the muscles of the ductus, which are sensitive to oxygen, acetylcholine, bradykinin, and endothelin. While the functional closure occurs within hours of birth, the anatomic closure may take several weeks. During the second stage, the ductus becomes fibrous and thus, there is no blood flow through it. Most infants have closure of the ductus within 2-3 weeks, and if the ductus remains patent after 8 weeks, it should be closed. Spontaneous closure of the ductus is very rare.

retrieved_docs.title: Transposition of the Great Arteries -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: As previously stated, TGA is challenging to detect on fetal ultrasound due to the lack of size differences between the ventricles. D-TGA can be diagnosed in utero using specialized views of the outflow tracts during fetal ultrasound. [21] Imaging is crucial for assessing d-TGA before and after surgical or nonsurgical interventions. The primary objectives of initial imaging are to confirm the diagnosis, detail the anatomy, evaluate hemodynamic abnormalities, assess the degree of mixing between pulmonary and systemic circulations, and identify any associated anomalies. Postprocedural or postsurgical imaging evaluates cardiac function and detects potential complications. The choice of imaging modalities depends on the patient's condition and the available technology and expertise at the medical facility. [22] Commonly used imaging techniques are listed below.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Thorax, Heart Ductus Arteriosus -- Clinical Significance -- Patent Ductus Arteriosus
retrieved_docs.content: Patent ductus arteriosus (PDA) is a condition in which the ductus arteriosus fails to close after birth. A PDA is one of the most common cardiac defects associated with congenital rubella syndrome and may severely impact an infant's quality of life. [9] The PDA leads to the generation of a left-to-right shunt since the blood pressures in the aorta are much higher than in the pulmonary artery. If allowed to remain patent, the pressure inside the pulmonary circuit will rise, leading to pulmonary hypertension. Failure to adequately address this pulmonary hypertension may lead to cyanosis and shunt reversal (Eisenmenger syndrome). Late complications of Eisenmenger syndrome include arrhythmias and sudden cardiac death. The development of this syndrome indicates irreversible changes to the cardiac and pulmonary vasculature, and lung/cardiac transplants are the only viable treatment option. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Ductus Venosus -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: The absence or dysfunction of the ductus venosus can lead to various disease states, fetal anomalies, and adverse outcomes. The absence of this shunt is associated with fetal hydrops, chromosomal aberration, in utero heart failure, and portal vein absence. [10] Abnormal blood flow through the ductus venosus, assessed using Doppler, increases the risk of cardiovascular defects, fetal growth restriction, renal anomaly, perinatal death, and aneuploidy. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Fetal Circulation -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: If a PDA is the only defect following delivery, and no other cardiac abnormalities are present, a prostaglandin synthesis inhibitor may be used to disrupt the prostaglandin production that is contributing to the maintained patency of the vessel. Indomethacin, a COX-2 inhibitor, has historically been the treatment of choice; ibuprofen and acetaminophen may also be efficacious options, although more research is needed. [9] Surgical closure may also be a consideration. Conversely, there are certain cyanotic congenital heart defects (for example, transposition of the great vessels) in which the circulatory system allows little or no deoxygenated blood delivery to the lungs. As a result, little or no oxygenated blood can be delivered systemically, causing cyanosis in peripheral tissues. In this case, therapeutically maintaining a patent ductus arteriosus allows some mixing of oxygenated and deoxygenated blood, allowing some amount of oxygenated blood into systemic circulation, which can assist in decreasing the severity of cyanosis in affected infants. To accomplish this, prostaglandin E1 may be administered to maintain patency of the ductus arteriosus until surgical correction of the underlying heart defect can be performed. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2099
retrieved_docs.content: Congenital vascular dilatation (تمدد الأوعية الدموية الفطري) is classified as a type of valvular heart disease (مرض الصمام القلبي). This condition involves abnormal dilation of blood vessels that is present from birth and is considered within the spectrum of diseases affecting heart valves. Recognizing congenital vascular dilatation as a valvular heart disease aids clinicians in diagnosing and monitoring potential valve dysfunction, altered hemodynamics, and related complications such as heart failure or arrhythmias, thereby guiding appropriate management and improving patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1724
retrieved_docs.content: Vascular dilatation (تمدد الأوعية الدموية) is a broad vascular condition that includes a specific subtype called congenital vascular dilatation (تمدد الأوعية الدموية الفطري). Congenital vascular dilatation is classified as a form of valvular heart disease (مرض الصمام القلبي). This hierarchical relationship indicates that the congenital form of vascular dilatation is both a child category of general vascular dilatation and a distinct type of valvular heart disease, highlighting its relevance in the diagnosis and management of cardiac valve disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 102
retrieved_docs.content: and cardiovascular complications are a subset of the overall perinatal condition spectrum. Collectively, these interrelated entities illustrate how gestational age, birthweight, and fetal growth trajectories define a network of classifications—from specific gestational milestones (29‑30 weeks) to broad perinatal disorder groups—providing a comprehensive framework for understanding, diagnosing, and managing health issues in liveborn infants.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 1 -- Community 196
retrieved_docs.content: not heart,” enabling clinicians to select appropriate catheter techniques, anticipate procedural steps, and integrate monitoring and therapeutic strategies across surgical, critical‑care, and fetal contexts.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2291
retrieved_docs.content: HEART: GENERAL AND MISCELLANEOUS OPERATIVE PROCEDURES is a broad surgical category that encompasses several specific cardiac interventions. Among its subprocedures are Temporary cardioassist with artificial heart, which involves the use of a mechanical device to provide short‑term circulatory support when the native heart cannot maintain adequate output, and Operation on heart and pericardium, which includes surgical techniques directed at repairing or modifying the myocardium, valves, coronary vessels, or the surrounding pericardial sac. Together, these child procedures illustrate the range of operative options available for managing acute cardiac failure, structural heart disease, and pericardial pathology within the overarching framework of general and miscellaneous heart surgery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1494
retrieved_docs.content: A gestational age of 29‑30 weeks completed is associated with a liveborn infant, indicating that pregnancies reaching this stage can result in a live birth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1756
retrieved_docs.content: The term “اضطراب جنيني” (fetal disorder) is mapped from “متلازمة الضائقة الجنينية” (fetal distress syndrome), indicating that the clinical entity described as fetal distress syndrome corresponds to or is represented by the label fetal disorder in certain contexts. This mapping clarifies terminology, ensuring consistent reference to the same condition across medical documentation and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 189
retrieved_docs.content: each condition as a potential adverse event, enabling systematic risk assessment, targeted monitoring, operative decision‑making, and coordinated maternal‑fetal and newborn care aimed at reducing morbidity and improving outcomes for both mother and infant.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1837
retrieved_docs.content: Allowed qualifier is identified as a pathology that can be applied to both مرض الصمام القلبي (heart valve disease) and تمدد الأوعية الدموية (vascular dilatation). Within the hierarchy of vascular abnormalities, تمدد الأوعية الدموية (vascular dilatation) has a child entity تمدد الأوعية الدموية الفطري (congenital vascular dilatation). Additionally, تمدد الأوعية الدموية الفطري (congenital vascular dilatation) is classified as a subtype of مرض الصمام القلبي (heart valve disease). These interconnections indicate that congenital vascular dilatation is both a specific form of vascular dilatation and a form of heart valve disease, and that the allowed qualifier can be used as a pathological descriptor for these conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1940
retrieved_docs.content: Fetal monitoring is classified as تنظيم الجنين.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 4 -- Community 2846
retrieved_docs.content: The concept of an allowed qualifier is applied to the prevention and control of mitral valve stenosis, threatened abortion, and fetal distress syndrome. Preventive and control strategies for mitral valve stenosis aim to limit valve narrowing and reduce cardiac complications. In cases of threatened abortion, early identification and appropriate interventions are essential to prevent progression to miscarriage and to protect maternal and fetal health. For fetal distress syndrome, vigilant monitoring and timely therapeutic measures are crucial to manage fetal compromise and improve outcomes for both mother and child. Integrating prevention and control measures for these conditions supports better patient care and reduces the risk of adverse events.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 1454
retrieved_docs.content: Mitral valve prolapse (تدلي الصمام المترالي) and congenital vascular dilatation (تمدد الأوعية الدموية الفطري) are both classified as forms of heart valve disease (مرض الصمام القلبي). Recognizing that these conditions fall under the broader category of heart valve disease is important for accurate diagnosis, risk assessment, and the selection of appropriate therapeutic strategies in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 4 -- Community 2873
retrieved_docs.content: The condition referred to as اضطراب جنيني (fetal disorder) is classified as a type of مضاعفات الحمل (pregnancy complication). Recognizing that fetal disorders are encompassed within the broader category of pregnancy complications helps clinicians identify, monitor, and manage these issues during prenatal care, ensuring appropriate assessment and intervention for both maternal and fetal health.
